text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY -- URGENT FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Patient: Gerald T. Holloway
DOB: [on file]
MRN: [on file]
Attending Oncologist: [on file]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mr. Holloway is a 59-year-old male with known Stage IVA KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS metastatic sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with hepatic and pulmonary metastases, presenting today for an urgent visit following restaging CT imaging that demonstrates progressive disease on his current second-line regimen of FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Holloway was initially diagnosed approximately 19 months ago with Stage IVA colorectal adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon (pT3 pN2a M1a) with synchronous hepatic metastases (four lesions, largest 3.1 cm in segment VI). His tumor harbors a KRAS p.G12D driver mutation (VAF 41% by NGS), APC and TP53 co-mutations, is microsatellite stable (MSS) by both IHC and NGS, TMB-low (6 mut/Mb), BRAF V600E wild-type, and HER2 non-amplified. These molecular characteristics preclude his eligibility for anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab) and for single-agent immune checkpoint blockade.

Following laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with R0 resection performed approximately 5 weeks after diagnosis (final pathology pT3 pN2a, 3/18 nodes, LVI and PNI present), he was started on first-line mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, LV 400 mg/m2, 5-FU bolus 400 mg/m2 + 5-FU CI 2400 mg/m2 over 46 hours, bevacizumab 5 mg/kg, q2w) with intent to assess liver-directed resection after cytoreduction. He achieved a meaningful hepatic response by cycle 6 restaging (all four liver metastases decreased ~40%), and further deepened to ~55% reduction at cycle 10 restaging. However, cumulative oxaliplatin-related peripheral sensory neuropathy became dose-limiting at cycle 10 (grade 2-3 -- persistent bilateral hand and foot paresthesias with significant functional impairment), and oxaliplatin was discontinued. He transitioned to maintenance 5-FU/bevacizumab.

During maintenance, the hepatic lesions remained reasonably stable, but restaging CT at approximately 10 months from treatment start demonstrated radiographic progression -- modest growth of all four hepatic metastases and appearance of two new right-sided pulmonary nodules (RLL 0.8 cm and RUL 0.5 cm) with a concurrent rise in CEA from 22 to 41 ng/mL. At that time, hepatic resection was deferred given the emergence of bilateral pulmonary metastatic disease, and he was transitioned to second-line therapy.

He was started on FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab (irinotecan 180 mg/m2, LV 400 mg/m2, 5-FU bolus/CI per FOLFOX schedule, ramucirumab 8 mg/kg, q2w) approximately 10.5 months from initial diagnosis. His course on this regimen has been complicated by grade 2 diarrhea (managed with loperamide as needed), grade 2 alopecia, and a significant episode of grade 3 hypertension at cycles 8-9, requiring ramucirumab to be held for one cycle and intensification of his antihypertensive regimen (amlodipine uptitrated to 10 mg daily, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg added). His UGT1A1 genotype was found to be *1/*28 (heterozygous), and he has been monitored closely for delayed-onset diarrhea throughout this regimen.

Initially, FOLFIRI/ramucirumab produced a partial response by cycle 6 restaging (hepatic lesions down ~17-23%, RLL nodule slightly decreased, CEA falling from 41 to 26 ng/mL). Restaging at cycle 12 (approximately 6 months on second-line therapy, 16 months from diagnosis) confirmed stable disease with continued disease control and CEA at 22 ng/mL. However, today I am reviewing the most recent restaging CT performed after cycle 18 (approximately 19 months from initial diagnosis), which shows unambiguous progression: all four hepatic metastases have grown modestly beyond the prior nadir on this regimen (segment VI now 1.5 cm from nadir 1.3 cm; IVb 1.2 cm from 1.0 cm; segment II 0.6 cm from 0.5 cm; segment VII 0.4 cm from 0.3 cm), and the RLL pulmonary nodule has grown from 0.7 cm to 0.9 cm. No new sites of disease. The sum of target lesion diameters has increased approximately 23% above nadir. More compellingly, his CEA has risen sharply from 22 to 58 ng/mL since the last stable scan, and CA 19-9 has climbed from 19 to 47 U/mL. Taken together, this CT and the tumor marker trajectory confirm progressive disease on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab after approximately 9 months on this regimen.

He presents today accompanied by his wife for an urgent discussion regarding disease status and options for further treatment.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: Increased fatigue over the past 4-6 weeks, which he describes as more pronounced than previous treatment-related fatigue. No significant unintentional weight loss reported (wife corroborates, states he is eating reasonably well). Mild decreased appetite.
Gastrointestinal: Intermittent loose stools (1-3 per day, not frankly diarrhea at present), managed with occasional loperamide. No rectal bleeding. No abdominal pain. No nausea or vomiting at this visit.
Neurologic: Persistent mild numbness and tingling in bilateral feet; he rates this as largely stable and less bothersome than it was at its worst during oxaliplatin treatment. No new neurologic symptoms. No headache.
Respiratory: No new dyspnea, cough, or hemoptysis. Mild exertional fatigue with moderate activity.
Skin: Mild hair thinning (alopecia from irinotecan, not cosmetically distressing to patient at this point).
Musculoskeletal: No new bone pain, joint pain, or focal musculoskeletal complaints.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations. Blood pressure has been variable at home -- mostly in the 140s/90s range, acceptable on current antihypertensives.
Genitourinary: No dysuria, hematuria, or new urinary complaints.
Psychosocial: Patient reports feeling anxious about today's news. His wife is present and engaged in the discussion. The patient expresses that he wishes to continue active treatment "as long as there is something reasonable to try." No active depression or suicidal ideation.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
General: Alert and oriented, appearing mildly fatigued but in no acute distress. Appropriately dressed and groomed. Accompanied by wife.
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 152/94 mmHg (right arm, seated, repeated 148/92 mmHg); Heart rate 78 bpm and regular; Temperature 36.8 degrees C; Respiratory rate 16/minute; SpO2 97% on room air; Weight 81.4 kg (baseline ~84 kg at diagnosis -- modest weight loss over the course of treatment).
HEENT: Normocephalic. Mucous membranes moist. No oral mucositis today. No scleral icterus. Mild diffuse alopecia.
Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy palpated.
Chest/Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze, crackle, or consolidation.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Well-healed laparoscopic port sites from prior sigmoid colectomy. No palpable hepatomegaly or splenomegaly. No right upper quadrant tenderness. No palpable masses. Active bowel sounds.
Extremities: Symmetric. No lower extremity edema. No calf tenderness.
Neurologic: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves intact. Mild decrease in light touch sensation in bilateral feet to approximately mid-foot level (consistent with chronic oxaliplatin-related sensory neuropathy). Proprioception mildly diminished at great toe bilaterally. Grip strength normal. Gait steady on flat ground; Romberg mildly positive with eyes closed.
Skin: No rash, no palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia. Mild alopecia as noted.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (RECENT)
CBC: WBC 5,400/uL; ANC 2,800/uL; Hemoglobin 10.6 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia, consistent with chronic disease/ongoing chemotherapy); Platelets 178,000/uL.
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Sodium 138 mEq/L; Potassium 3.9 mEq/L; Creatinine 0.95 mg/dL (eGFR >60); Total bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL; ALT 61 U/L (mildly elevated, stable or slightly higher than last check); AST 55 U/L (mildly elevated); Alkaline phosphatase 112 U/L (mildly elevated, new slight increase -- worth monitoring).
Tumor Markers: CEA 58 ng/mL (rising sharply from 22 ng/mL at last stable scan 3 months ago); CA 19-9 47 U/mL (rising from 19 U/mL).
Coagulation: PT/INR within normal limits.
Urine protein/creatinine ratio: 0.18 (below threshold of concern for bevacizumab/ramucirumab-associated proteinuria).

---

IMAGING RESULTS
Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (most recent, performed approximately 2 weeks ago): As reviewed in detail above, this demonstrates progressive disease. The four hepatic metastases have each grown modestly relative to the nadir achieved on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab -- the largest, in segment VI, now measures 1.5 cm (up from 1.3 cm at the last stable scan 3 months prior), IVb measures 1.2 cm (up from 1.0 cm), segment II 0.6 cm (up from 0.5 cm), segment VII 0.4 cm (up from 0.3 cm). The right lower lobe pulmonary nodule has grown from 0.7 cm to 0.9 cm. The right upper lobe nodule is relatively stable at 0.5 cm. No new metastatic foci identified in the peritoneum, bones, or other viscera. The colectomy site appears unremarkable without local recurrence.

While the absolute size increases are individually modest, the pattern of synchronous growth across all sites coupled with the steep rise in CEA (nearly tripled from nadir) is clinically consistent with real disease progression and not measurement variability. This is not borderline -- this is a regimen that has run its course.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Holloway is a 59-year-old male with progressive metastatic sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma, KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS, BRAF wild-type, HER2 non-amplified, with hepatic and bilateral pulmonary metastases. He has now exhausted two lines of standard combination chemotherapy:
-- First line: mFOLFOX6 + bevacizumab (cycles 1-10 with oxaliplatin, then maintenance 5-FU/bevacizumab) -- achieved partial response, limited by cumulative neuropathy; progressed at approximately 10 months.
-- Second line: FOLFIRI + ramucirumab (12 cycles, approximately 9 months) -- achieved partial response initially, now progressing clinically and radiographically.

He retains an ECOG PS of 1 to 2 (I would characterize today as a 2, given the increased fatigue and some functional decline, though he remains ambulatory and partially self-caring), adequate end-organ function, and he and his family are motivated and engaged in pursuing continued active treatment.

Given his molecular profile and treatment history, the following third-line options were discussed at length with the patient and his wife today:

1. TAS-102 (trifluridine/tipiracil, Lonsurf): An oral cytotoxic agent approved in the third-line and beyond for metastatic CRC. Data from the RECOURSE trial demonstrated modest but statistically significant improvement in overall survival (~7.1 months vs. ~5.3 months with placebo) in a heavily pre-treated population. Toxicity profile includes myelosuppression (neutropenia is the primary concern), fatigue, and nausea -- generally manageable. Does not require KRAS wild-type status. Given Mr. Holloway's adequate bone marrow reserve and organ function, this would be feasible.

2. Regorafenib (Stivarga): An oral multikinase inhibitor with activity in refractory mCRC. Also third-line and beyond indication. The ReDOS dosing strategy (dose escalation starting at 80 mg daily) is associated with improved tolerability compared to the standard 160 mg starting dose. Primary toxicities include hand-foot skin reaction, fatigue, hypertension, and hepatotoxicity. Given Mr. Holloway's existing hypertension and borderline liver enzyme elevation, this would require close monitoring and may be less ideal from a tolerability standpoint.

3. Clinical trial -- KRAS G12D-directed investigational therapy: The patient's tumor harbors a confirmed KRAS p.G12D mutation, which has been an area of intense drug development. Several early-phase trials are actively enrolling patients with KRAS G12D-mutant solid tumors. This represents a biologically rational approach and potentially the most compelling option for a molecularly selected patient like Mr. Holloway. I have identified a Phase I/II study at our regional cancer center evaluating a selective KRAS G12D inhibitor in patients with solid tumors including colorectal cancer. I discussed this at length, including the investigational nature of the therapy, the need to re-biopsy for correlative studies as per the trial protocol, and the logistical implications of treatment at an outside center.

After discussion, Mr. Holloway expressed strong interest in the clinical trial option. He and his wife understand the investigational nature and are willing to travel to the regional center. He is also amenable to TAS-102 as a bridge or fallback if trial screening is unsuccessful.

Plan:
-- FOLFIRI/ramucirumab to be discontinued effective today given confirmed progressive disease.
-- Referral placed urgently to the clinical trial unit at the regional cancer center for screening and eligibility assessment for the KRAS G12D inhibitor trial. I have contacted the PI directly and will forward all relevant records, molecular reports, and imaging.
-- Re-biopsy of an accessible lesion (the right lower lobe pulmonary nodule is the most accessible site) will be required per the trial protocol for fresh correlative tissue. A CT-guided biopsy will be arranged through the trial site.
-- If clinical trial screening is not successful or enrollment is delayed beyond 4-6 weeks, we will initiate TAS-102 as third-line therapy in the interim.
-- Goals of care discussion conducted today. Patient and wife have been informed regarding the progressive nature of his disease after two lines of chemotherapy. He understands that his cancer is not curable with available standard therapies, but he wishes to continue active treatment with the goal of disease control and quality of life preservation. A palliative care consultation has been placed to run in parallel with active treatment planning -- patient is open to this.
-- Blood pressure: Today's reading is concerning at 152/94 mmHg. Given that ramucirumab is being discontinued, we will observe over the coming weeks for improvement. If hypertension persists at this level off anti-angiogenic therapy, I will work with his primary care physician to reassess his antihypertensive regimen. Continue lisinopril 20 mg, amlodipine 10 mg, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg for now.
-- Residual neuropathy: Continue monitoring. No specific interventions needed at this time; grade 1 residual sensory neuropathy from prior oxaliplatin is expected to persist but is tolerable.
-- Mild anemia (Hgb 10.6 g/dL): Does not require transfusion at this time. Consistent with anemia of chronic disease in the setting of ongoing malignancy. Will recheck CBC at next visit.
-- Mild transaminase elevation (ALT 61, AST 55, ALP 112): Likely multifactorial -- chronic disease, residual treatment effect, and possibly early metastatic hepatic parenchymal involvement. Will monitor closely; if rising ALP or bilirubin emerges, repeat imaging of the liver and further evaluation warranted.
-- Next visit: Follow-up within 2-3 weeks for clinical trial screening update and transition planning, or sooner if clinically urgent.
-- Patient tolerated today's lengthy and difficult conversation well. His wife is engaged and supportive. He was provided written educational materials on clinical trial participation. Social work and financial counseling referral offered for assistance with travel and logistical support related to off-site trial participation.

Estimated ECOG performance status at today's visit: 1-2.
Cancer status: Present, progressing.
Treatment status: Off active systemic therapy (FOLFIRI/ramucirumab discontinued today); transition to clinical trial or TAS-102 planned.

Time spent: 65 minutes, greater than 50% of which was devoted to counseling and coordination of care.

Signed: Attending Medical Oncologist
[Dictated and electronically signed]
[cc: Primary care physician, Hepatobiliary Surgery, Palliative Care, Regional Cancer Center Clinical Trials Unit]
